item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel pity for the D class who almost made it through.

I feel pity that he lost his family and all the good and bad choices HE had to live through.

In my head canon he survived. As he looked into the glass pane and let the skip move his mind to somewhere even happier.
Either that, or it is cruel. :(

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Hmm… I don't think the tale is really related to this article, and the article isn't focused on D-Class anyway, so I'd prefer to decline this edit.

Thanks for the proposal!

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: author vetoed edit

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

In the last few lines of the interview, I propose linking to the tale Right?. I feel like this tale gives the last lines a greater amount of context, and make it easier to understand for newbies who don't know the whole "D-Class headcanon" yet. In addition, they fit thematically.

Editing this:

"You released me after the SCP-███ incident. I was free to go. Why am I back here?"

To this:

"You released me after the SCP-███ incident. I was free to go. Why am I back here?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Scary…
As i read the comments i can fairly imagine some experiment log, with researcher loyal to foundation.
Yes, the chance to lose the researcher is high. Yes, getting mind of same researcher from parallel universe could be worth it.
Unfortunately Foundation surely have a lot of reliable version to do so.
I feel pity for the D class who almost made it through.
Also i feel a surge of fear and disgust now. But would like and probably enjoy temporal version of this pane personally.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely! I really appreciate your article!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It just seems very formulaic. Mysterious item found in mysterious place with mysterious events having seemed to take place.

Yeah, I can feel that. I might have had more stuff in an earlier version during the draft process, but it's entirely possible I trimmed it out for brevity and "this could have happened near yooooooouuuu"-ness. Perhaps it can be expanded in a tale!

There were other panes of glass on site, but why iron precipitate?

Spoilers! (Well, so much as I can remember.) There might have been some sort of blood ritual involved. I think. I'll have to dig up my writing notes to check sometime.

the comment carved into the stretcher provokes such a powerful image that really unifies the other elements of the article. I loved that!

I'm glad you did! I tried to end with that, since that was the strongest thing I had in the work.

Thanks for the comments, I really appreciate your insights on this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure I had any specific idea in mind for an ending when I mentioned the lack of depth in the recovery log, unfortunately. It just seems very formulaic. Mysterious item found in mysterious place with mysterious events having seemed to take place.

When I read the recovery logs, it seems quite discordant, and I have trouble tying the specific elements together. For example, it seems it was recovered from a research facility with no other indications as to what they did there. There were other panes of glass on site, but why iron precipitate? We have 5 bodies left at the scene, presumably they were all matched to the iron precipitate plates. Seems like these things are trying to lead us somewhere. Perhaps adding a recovery site would add a few more hints/clues/elements for the reader to ponder.

I would not advocate removing the recovery logs by any means, as the comment carved into the stretcher provokes such a powerful image that really unifies the other elements of the article. I loved that!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't feel that leaving out the recovery site adds anything to the overall story being told here.

I left out the recovery site because I didn't have a specific place in mind when I wrote the article. I was considering that it would be a densely-populated city in Asia, but the partial log and writing on the glass are both in English.

A bland ending that is seen in so many SCP documents, and a missed opportunity to add more depth

What would you suggest to add that depth?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely adore the concept here. A short and sweet document detailing a small break in reality. Doesn't overstay it's welcome, and pulls a bit on the heartstrings.

Addendum 2885-2: SCP-2885 was recovered on ██-██-██ in the ███████ Facility, in █████, ████████.

I don't feel that leaving out the recovery site adds anything to the overall story being told here. I was mostly disappointed with the recovery notes until the message carved into the stretcher. A bland ending that is seen in so many SCP documents, and a missed opportunity to add more depth to an already interesting article.

Overall, +1

item.title: Re: Author Notes
item.content:encoded: Thanks for creating this, Zyn. This is much better presentation of my idea than I had, or probably could have made. I know this thank you is a bit late, but I'm really glad this exists.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This really dragged on, with the dreadfully boring descriptions of how the hallucination takes over your body slowly.

When I read the note, I assumed that if you looked into it long enough, a worse-off version of you would crawl out and kick your ass, then shove your unconscious body back through. I was mostly disappointed that this wasn't anything as cool as that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I sort of tried to incorporate both the paranoia of "you never know" with the adage "count your blessings".

There's probably another version of you in a parallel universe who's king of the world, but there might be another one who's exactly like you except just a little bit happier… but is there a "you" that more deserves having what another "you" has?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The real horror of this one, for me, is in the idea of being the one replaced.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hate this concept and am done with the wiki for probably the next hour or so at least.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the object appears to imbue not-in-the-know individuals with sensitive knowledge

potential spoilers
hide

It's a toss-up, really. There's no guarantee that the better-off alternate you that this swaps your mind with actually has sensitive knowledge. The only guarantee is that "they" are happier with their lot in life than "you" are.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I haven't been able to do more than glance over this (my eyes are tired, and I'll probably read it tomorrow), but I'd just like to say that I really liked that you've mixed the format up a bit by putting inscriptions, etc. in a box near the start of the description. It's a nice change from the standard 'lengthy inscriptions go at the end, in an addendum' set-up most articles go with. It's a good reminder that the SCP format is a format, not a formula, and that there's no real reason to rigidly adhere to some of the common article-writing conventions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it, a good one which have to stay!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What I would not give to have this. So, so many things that happened one way that could've been another, where I wouldn't be in this position. +1 for unintentional feels.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah Zyn. I can't say I enjoyed this so much, but the hook was necessary or else the article would've been considered boring. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was a well-written article, and tells a good story through the experiment log. I find it a bit hard to believe, however, that anyone suffering from such a delusional state would continue to follow original instructions after hours upon hours of hallucinating. I also wonder if the containment procedures couldn't be a bit beefier, especially since the object appears to imbue not-in-the-know individuals with sensitive knowledge, such as SCP Site layouts.

That being said it's still a good read. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just as gloriously heart-wrenching as ever.

Yay.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This bears the Zyn Seal of Quality. +1

item.title: Author Notes
item.content:encoded:
Many thanks to LongWayHome and Palaios in the forums, and djkaktus, ihpkmn, Tuomey, Zolgamax, and Mastersarge in chat for reading this over!

This is a rewrite of Window to Another World, by Dax_Symbiont. Permission for the rewrite was given on the mainsite, when the original was submitted and later being voted on for deletion.

Potential spoilers
SPOILERS

I really liked Dax’s original idea of a pane of glass that effectively let someone’s mind travel to someone else’s and take it over, essentially stealing someone’s life. I thought it over a few nights and it occurred to me, what with all the potential parallel universes out there, how many alternate versions of ourselves are living it up? Maybe they made just a few different choices, maybe they’re not ridiculously more happy, but chances are good there are a bunch of other “you”s out there who are happier.

So what’s the harm in living “your” life? Giving yourself another chance? Getting ahead of the game and dropping yourself into a place where you did make all the right choices, or at least most of them? Whoever created this skip (and the other prototypes) probably had these questions in mind. Question is though… are the others really “you”? What are “you” entitled to?

The end note, with the empty stretcher? Not everyone is totally fine with the switch, as can be expected. Nearly all of the “better-off” versions don’t make it to the other side when the switch happens, but some do.
